hero.default: I specialize in designing and deploying agentic workflows. My recent work involves partnering with health-tech organizations to build conversational agent companions, alongside developing specialized tools that optimize AI context management and data privacy. I focus on the entire lifecycle—from the initial problem statement to full-scale deployment.
hero.headline: I specialize in designing and deploying agentic workflows. My recent work involves partnering with health-tech organizations to build conversational agent companions, alongside developing specialized tools that optimize AI context management and data privacy. I focus on the entire lifecycle—from the initial problem statement to full-scale deployment.
hero.supporting: Plan-Flow-Reflect and hybrid routing are not slogans here. They are the operating constraints behind how I build systems that know when to act, when to verify, and when to stop.

about.default: This section explains the operating model behind the builds. The goal is to show how I think about orchestration, routing, and execution layers before the projects arrive as proof.
about.headline: I am not positioning AI as a chat interface. I am positioning it as an execution engine that can plan, coordinate tools, and preserve context across real workflows.
about.summary: The key architectural bias is context management. Good systems do not just generate answers. They deliver the right information to the right model at the right moment.
about.center: Agentic AI sits at the center because the value is not in isolated tools. It is in designing how those tools collaborate under one execution model.

skills.Java: Java signals systems discipline. It is where I learned to think in strong abstractions, maintainability, and production-grade service design.
skills.Python: Python is the fastest bridge from idea to automation. It powers orchestration, backend logic, and the agent workflows that need to move quickly without losing rigor.
skills.Node.js: Node.js is where I build event-driven execution layers and interfaces that need to react in real time across browser and backend boundaries.
skills.React: React is the presentation layer for complex systems. I use it to surface architecture, state, and interaction in a way that feels deliberate rather than decorative.
skills.n8n: n8n represents my bias for visible orchestration. It turns multi-step automation into something observable, editable, and resilient instead of hidden inside scripts.
skills.TypeScript: TypeScript lets the interface and orchestration layers stay explicit. It reduces ambiguity in systems where state shape and tool contracts matter.
skills.PostgreSQL: PostgreSQL is where operational truth lives. I rely on it when a system needs relational integrity, predictable state, and enterprise-grade persistence.
skills.Supabase: Supabase is useful when speed matters but I still want structured data, auth primitives, and developer velocity without giving up a real database.
skills.Tailwind CSS: Tailwind CSS is how I keep interfaces expressive without drifting into styling entropy. It helps the UI stay systemized and intentional.
skills.Docker: Docker matters because systems should travel cleanly. Packaging the environment is part of the architecture, not an afterthought after the code works.
skills.Git: Git is the memory layer for engineering. It preserves intent, iteration, and accountability across how systems actually evolve.
skills.Chrome: Chrome points to browser-resident architecture. It is where I build tools that live close to user behavior, DOM state, and execution context.

projects.default: Most portfolios show the what. This section is designed to surface the why, so each project card becomes a guided explanation of the architectural pivot that made the system work.

thread-navigator.default: I built this to solve the context tax. It is an engineering bridge between isolated LLMs, ensuring reasoning moves as fast as you do, with total privacy baked into the architecture.

signals.Context Teleportation Protocol: Copying is easy; preserving reasoning is hard. This protocol packages intent into a context packet for instant injection and removes the tax of switching models.
signals.Zero-Server Architecture: Data leakage kills adoption. Everything lives in Chrome storage with no backend, no databases, and no APIs. That privacy model is why engineers trusted it immediately.
signals.Cross-Platform DOM Interoperability: Walled gardens lacked APIs, so I used the DOM. The parser navigates HTML trees across platforms and turns structured chaos into portable context.
signals.Client-Side Execution Only: Local execution removes latency and infrastructure overhead. By leaning on Chrome runtime, the tool scales without servers, operational cost, or data residency risk.

sakhi.default: Most healthcare copilots optimize for answers. I optimized for failure modes. Sakhi is a zero-trust clinical agent where every architectural layer exists to prevent unsafe reasoning from reaching the patient.

signals.4-Layer Hallucination Shield: Most bots hope LLMs stay on track. I built a stream-level auditor scanning generated tokens against grounded source material. If the stream drifts, it aborts.
signals.Confidence-Gated Hybrid Routing: Local SLMs handle routine queries for low latency and zero cloud exposure. Complex cases hit GPT only after PII scrubbing, balancing privacy, safety, and cost.
signals.Self-Improving Knowledge Flywheel: Every blocked query is logged. Failures are re-ingested as new HRAG nodes with composite embeddings, so the system gets safer as it fails.
signals.Stream Abort on Drift Detection: The agent validates reasoning while it streams. If entities or claims detach from approved evidence, token emission stops before the answer leaves the system.

presales.default: This was not about making sales research nicer. It was about collapsing an entire pre-sales operating model into one autonomous pipeline where enrichment, reasoning, and delivery happen as a single system.

signals.Multi-Agent Orchestration (n8n): I traded god-scripts for an observable agent pipeline via n8n. If one stage degrades, the others can still progress with clear state visibility and retry boundaries.
signals.Dual-Schema RAG Engine: Enterprise RFPs mix facts and narratives. PostgreSQL holds strict relational truth while VectorDB handles conceptual retrieval. They merge at the UI into one coherent draft.
signals.15-Minute Automated Workflow: The system compresses discovery, transcription, and deck generation into one accelerated session, turning a multi-day prep cycle into an operational sprint.
signals.Webhook-Triggered Automation Pipeline: The workflow wakes up on external events, pushes context through specialized stages, and produces deliverables without forcing humans to babysit each handoff.

blogs.default: The writing section extends the proof. These posts capture the reasoning patterns behind the systems so the viewer sees repeatable judgment, not isolated project outcomes.
blogs.intro: These are not tutorials. They are field notes from systems that broke, scaled, or exposed a pattern worth keeping.

items.ai-for-vizag-journey: This post is about momentum creation. A small opportunity became a room of 200 plus people because the artifact was concrete enough to travel on its own.
items.thread-navigator-v1-3: This piece reframes prompt repetition as a systems problem. The interesting move was shifting from manually managing context to engineering reusable inputs.
items.ai-workflow-wall: This article breaks down the local-first context engine behind LLM workflow recovery. The real point is that workflow pain is usually a context retrieval problem disguised as UX friction.
